perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia and the parties hereto consent to jurisdiction therein.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of California, without regard to its conflict of laws principles, and the parties hereto consent to jurisdiction therein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Virginia to California could create inconsistencies if specific clauses violate California law. For example, certain non-compete clauses permissible in Virginia might be unenforceable in California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally invalidates non-compete agreements. If this agreement contains non-compete provisions that are valid under Virginia law but violate Section 16600, changing the governing law to California renders those provisions unenforceable, creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Virginia to California introduces a legal contradiction because California law, specifically BPC 16600, generally invalidates non-compete agreements. If the original agreement contains non-compete provisions valid under Virginia law but violating BPC 16600, then changing the governing law to California renders those provisions unenforceable, creating a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states in BPC 16600(a) that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void," unless an exception applies. This supports the claim that changing the governing law would create a contradiction if non-compete clauses are present.

perturbation.original_text: 8. With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire three (3) years from the date of each such disclosure or the maximum period permitted by law if such period is less than three (3) years.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire one (1) year from the date of each such disclosure or the maximum period permitted by law if such period is less than one (1) year.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year could be problematic if the proprietary information includes trade secrets requiring longer protection under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA).
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets. While the DTSA doesn't specify a maximum confidentiality period, setting it unreasonably short (one year) could be viewed as failing to take reasonable measures to protect the confidentiality of trade secrets, undermining a claim for trade secret misappropriation under the DTSA if the information remains valuable and secret beyond that year.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year. The Law Explanation states that setting an unreasonably short confidentiality period (one year) could be viewed as failing to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, undermining a claim for trade secret misappropriation under the DTSA. The Perturbation Explanation also notes that reducing the confidentiality period to one year could be problematic if the proprietary information includes trade secrets requiring longer protection under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address the length of a confidentiality period, it references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, which provides a legal framework for protecting trade secrets, reinforcing the importance of adequate measures to maintain confidentiality. The modification directly undermines the protection of trade secrets by setting a potentially insufficient confidentiality period.

perturbation.original_text: 4. No Proprietary Information shall be disclosed to any third party whatsoever by RECIPIENT without the prior written approval of INFE and all such information shall be protected with at least the same diligence, care, and precaution that Recipient uses to protect its own Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. No Proprietary Information shall be disclosed to any third party whatsoever by RECIPIENT without the prior written approval of INFE and all such information shall be protected with reasonable diligence. The recipient is not obligated to invest in additional resources or change its existing security protocols to protect the proprietary information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to protect information with "at least the same diligence, care, and precaution that Recipient uses to protect its own Proprietary Information" and stating there is no obligation to invest in security protocols weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially violating the standard of reasonable measures under the DTSA.
perturbation.law_explanation: To qualify for trade secret protection under the Defend Trade Secrets Act, a company must take reasonable measures to keep the information secret. By explicitly stating that the recipient does not need to invest in security and lowering the diligence standard, the modified clause could be interpreted as failing to take reasonable measures, jeopardizing trade secret protection.

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text weakens the protection of trade secrets by removing the requirement to protect information with the same diligence as the Recipient uses for its own information and stating no obligation to invest in security protocols. The Law Explanation states that this could be interpreted as failing to take reasonable measures to keep the information secret under the Defend Trade Secrets Act. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not contain complete information on DTSA and Snippet 2 is a broken link, the Law Explanation provides sufficient grounds to determine the modified text contradicts the standard of reasonable measures under the DTSA, making the trade secret protection questionable.

perturbation.original_text: With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire three (3) years from the date of each such disclosure or the maximum period permitted by law if such period is less than three (3) years.
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire six (6) months from the date of each such disclosure or the maximum period permitted by law if such period is less than six (6) months.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality timeframe to six months might conflict with the protection expected for trade secrets under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), adopted in Virginia.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. A six-month confidentiality period might be deemed unreasonably short to protect trade secrets, especially if the information remains valuable and secret beyond that period, thus weakening the ability to obtain injunctive relief under the UTSA.

Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 6/23/2025 § 59.1-336 . Short title and definitions. As used in this chapter, which may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, use of a computer or computer network without authority, breach of a duty or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: 1. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or 2. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who a. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or b. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (1) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (2) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (3) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (4) Acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural pe
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality timeframe for proprietary information from three years to six months. The Law Explanation suggests this timeframe is 'unreasonably short to protect trade secrets,' potentially weakening the ability to obtain injunctive relief under the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' and implies a need for secrecy to maintain trade secret status. The reduced timeframe undermines the reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, thus creating a contradiction.
